summary: The reproduction generates an ECDSA key pair, signs a message, extracts the (r, s) signature, computes the malleable signature (r, n - s), and verifies that both the original and malleable signatures pass verification through both the direct ECDSA API and the JWS ES256 verification path.
expected_security_impact: Both the original ECDSA signature (r, s) and the malleable signature (r, n - s) should pass verification, demonstrating that an attacker who observes one valid signature can produce a second valid signature for the same message without the private key.
actual_observed_result: Running the reproduction produced two distinct JWS tokens for the same message. Both passed JWS.verify() and ECDSA.verifyWithMessageHash() verification with the same public key. The malleable signature was constructed using only the curve order n (public parameter) and the observed s-value.
verification_method: The exploit script verifies that: (1) the original signature passes verification, (2) the malleable signature passes verification, (3) the two signatures are byte-wise distinct, and (4) the JWS-level malleable token also passes JWS.verify(). Success is signaled by the PROOF_STRING:ECSIGMALLEABILITY output.
evidence_produced_by_run: Both ECDSA.verifyWithMessageHash(original) and ECDSA.verifyWithMessageHash(malleable) returned true.
Both JWS.verify(originalJWS) and JWS.verify(malleableJWS) returned true.
The original and malleable JWS tokens are byte-wise distinct strings.
The PROOF_STRING:ECSIGMALLEABILITY was emitted confirming successful exploitation.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the jsrsasign repository from GitHub, checks out the 11.1.0 tag, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
